summary: Gruber dismantles the common claim that the iTunes Store is merely a loss-leader for iPod sales, showing Apple earns roughly 30 cents per song on billions of annual sales — serious revenue. He argues Amazon's entry into DRM-free MP3 sales makes perfect sense given Amazon's expertise in high-volume, low-margin retail. The real story is that music labels finally realized the iPod drives iTunes sales (not vice versa), and that only by dropping their DRM requirements could they enable competition with iTunes. Universal and EMI's willingness to sell DRM-free music through Amazon is less about Amazon's technology and more about the labels' belated strategic capitulation.
key_points: Apple keeps ~30 cents per song sold, generating hundreds of millions in annual revenue — the iTunes Store is not a loss-leader
Amazon's core competency in high-volume, low-margin online retail makes it a natural fit for music downloads
The labels' insistence on DRM was the real barrier to iTunes competition — Amazon's store became possible only when Universal and EMI agreed to sell DRM-free
Universal is taking a spiteful approach by offering DRM-free music through Amazon but not through iTunes
Amazon's branding choice of 'MP3' over 'Music' cleverly signals openness, compatibility, and DRM-free downloads
money_quotes: So why is Amazon even bothering with a music download store, given that "everyone knows" the iTunes Store is a loss-leader that Apple offers just to sell more iPods? Because that's bullshit.
In short, while Apple doesn't earn much at all on the sale of a single song, they make up for it in volume.
Well, perhaps Amazon can find a book or something about how to build a successful high-volume online store.
People buy iPods because they love them. If your music doesn't play on iPods, it isn't going to sell.
The hard part for Amazon wasn't building the store. The hard part was getting two of the four major music labels to allow them to do it.
Apple's FairPlay DRM isn't (at least primarily) some sort of lock-in scheme to force people to buy iPods; FairPlay was a requirement stipulated by the labels.
Using "MP3" instead of "music" emphasizes two things: that this is about downloads as opposed to CDs, and it helps establish the openness and compatibility of the music they're selling.
key_insight: The iTunes Store was never a loss-leader — it's a profitable high-volume business, and Amazon's entry was only possible because music labels finally accepted that dropping DRM was the only way to compete with Apple's ecosystem dominance.
